posts.author: cvs
posts.text: ITC limited dividend. — Hmmm… The short-delivery is on the seller’s side, right? Not sure how “cash segment” and “retail customer” on the buyer’s side, can filter out sellers who can short-deliver. AFAIK, retail buyers cannot select the seller nor verify in advance that the seller has shares ready for delivery. Correct. But this does not involve guaranteeing that shares will be credited (let alone the dividend payment, which is a moot point, if shares itself aren’t credited). Cash settlement is a distinct possibility in case of short-delivery. More likely? sure. Will? eh… Again not necessarily…

posts.author: neha1101
posts.text: ITC limited dividend. — I said in the cash segment and for retail customers. Market depth indicates the buyers and sellers that are available at a price range and how liquid the stock is. So if someone really wants to buy and if the price range matches, the transaction will match and he will get the shares executed. Short delivery is for the clearinghouse to worry about and not for a retail buyer in the cash segment. What happens if the seller does not deliver is a backend/system/clearinghouse issue and not the buyer’s. The buyer will get the shares if the company is a liquid stock like ITC.

posts.author: S_Kumar3
posts.text: ITC limited dividend. — @cvs Oh ok got it.

posts.author: cvs
posts.text: ITC limited dividend. — Dividends above a threshold (2%) may be treated as an extraordinary corporate action. If the dividend is large relative to the stock price, NSE may adjust futures/option contracts. A dividend of 8 INR on a stock priced at ~300 INR is >2.5%, so it is very likely to fall into the “extraordinary dividend” category. For extra-ordinary dividends, which are at and above 2% of the market value of the underlying security, the Strike Price would be adjusted. Source: NSE - National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. If the futures contract is adjusted downward for the dividend, then th…

posts.author: cvs
posts.text: ITC limited dividend. — Not necessarily. Market depth does not mean delivery is guaranteed. It only shows orders available for execution. A buy order can get matched to a sell order, but the seller may still fail to deliver shares on settlement. That is precisely what a short delivery is. Very likely no. Atleast not directly. Since, you do not have the necessary shares in your posession on the key date. A broker/clearing corporation invovled may indirectly compensate the individual for such corporate action benefits that the individual may have missed out on. But, i do not find a common author…

posts.author: Investor_Prad
posts.text: While the results of ITC where out today, the below is what my news feed looked like, image 349×339 55.9 KB So like any sane person would do, I went into their financials and looked at what went wrong image 846×235 36.6 KB The company had booked a sales gain of 15k crores which boasted their previous year (FY25) earnings, meanwhile, the normal course of the business is inline with the previous years with considerable growth in the topline as well as the profit level. With more liquidity and volatility in the market, it becomes really pruden to see how these kind of news essentially plays out!…
